All right, you're back and guess what? It's time for a sort of bonus lesson. This is really just massively important content. I wouldn't say it's a bonus lesson. It's a real lesson. But I like to always end with a bonus, right? This is a crucial one. It's on following up, meaning what do you do when you get to that point in your looping process where you got to say, you know what? I've gone about as far as I can go. It makes strategic sense that what the person catch their breath, become more certain their own, check with who they have to check with, right? And eventually, you know, follow up with that person again and again. Let me just tell you something. You must have a relentless follow up campaign. You must. Just the way things are in this world is you're not going to want to, it doesn't make sense to apply that much pressure on every single call. I'm a big believer in following up. That being said, there's following up and then there's following up the right way. I don't want you just following up indiscriminately and wasting your time and following up because you'd rather follow up on someone who you've spoken to before versus either picking up the phone or knocking on a new door or going to a new business. So that's the wrong type of follow up. The right type of follow up is going after real legitimate buyers who are in that sale cycle that you've created. They're somewhere in the middle and need some time to get more certain on their own, check with other people and so forth. How do you follow up on those people the correct way? Well, let me tell you something. If you master the art of the follow up and do it the right way, it could amount to 30 or 40%, even 50% of your actual sales. So this is nothing to be sneezed at. This is a crucial lesson here. And there were so many different strategies and tactics that go into correct follow up and decided that rather than trying to organize them all into a bunch of different lessons, I do it in a way that was more fun, which is why I'm at the desk again. I'm going to get interviewed. All right. So I asked Dr. McCool while she was here doing the standards interviews. Do me a favor. I want you to go through my content on following up, give me all those deep questions and kind of peel back layers of successful follow up and let's create that last lesson of looping and turn into the most awesome follow up training ever. And that's what this is. Let's cut to that footage right now with Dr. McCool and we'll go through follow up and we're done. I'll see you on the other side. So the thing that I noticed about follow ups is that it seems like there needs to be a balance struck between knowing when it's appropriate to follow up and when people should just go after new leads. So can you talk a little bit about that balance and what people should know? Yeah. That's basically the million dollar question was. I mean, there's two types of salespeople out there. There are those salespeople that hate to call call and those people will do anything not to so they'll follow up with the same leads long after anybody that was taking things through a lot. I would say, you know, I might as well not call them because they ain't buying it or not picking up. It's a dead end. And there's those that are the exact opposite where they love to call call and they love to blaze the trail and maybe not call call, but they love to do second calls. In other words, so in a two call system, you know, the initial call was a cold call and then you sent them out some information or whatever it might be you scheduled the second call and that next call that's your high percentage at first time you get a maybe close, right? And then perhaps the call after that knows to a second and third call represent your best shots to close. Now that being said, I know that there's lots of articles out there, conventional wisdom that says, oh, you know, most sales occur after the seventh call. That's just not, it's not true. Now, obviously in some specific industries, yes, that is true. It might take six times for a prospect to have all the multiple decision makers lined up and so forth, right? But that's, I would say, I want to call the exception to the rule as much as that to say that's what you should be doing is just pure nonsense. Let me give you an example. Okay. In the financial services industry, if you're dealing with average everyday people, they don't buy on the second or third call, they're not going to be buying from you. They're just, they're just not. It's a very low hit ratio for individuals. Now if you're calling companies going up to B to B, yeah, well, then there's a different, you know, paradigm because there's multiple decision makers involved. So I guess my point is that I don't want you to think, I want you to think that there's some hard and fast rule that you got to keep calling someone eight times because most sales will give up after three. So the first thing to consider here is, well, what industry you would, if you're in an industry where there's multiple decision makers, where it's a product that requires considerable amount of integration, where you're just not just buying a standalone product that integrate into other systems, right? That type of product will typically take a lot more follow up until you can corral all the different decision makers to comfortable consensus because it's not about one person having the three tens line. There's not multiple people having the three tens line though. You get it? So that will require more calls. So here's the, the, really the shortest way to answer this question. If I can answer anything shortly, right? Is that the first step in the process to identify what is your true sales cycle? Meaning in the realistic world, when you actually take the outcomes to the end of all the people who have ever bought from you, when are they buying? And not just you, but company wife working to tell you, what's the average with good salesmen, not as salesmen aren't as strong, right? What's the average? Well, the average number of calls is they buy in the third or fourth call, right? And if you notice that after the fifth call, no one's ever buying, well, if you're following up that fifth call, you're out of your mind. You're just, what you're doing is you're avoiding cold calling. You're avoiding getting on. You just want to waste your time. You appear to be busy when you're not doing anything that's productive. Okay. So that's the first part about follow up is that understanding what the truth is about your industry and your sales cycle. On one extreme, you'd have something, something like Stratton, where we were doing very wealthy people. When you pitched that first time, didn't close the chances of them closing on next call were like really, really small. And if they didn't close that next call, they never would buy ever, like not even once, right? So what would you do there? Well, in that situation, obviously, the appropriate follow up would be limited. And also when you are setting up that next call, especially after the second, let's say you didn't close on the second call, first call, you set them information. You scheduled the callback, you call back, you pitched them an idea. They said, let me think about it. You couldn't overcome that through looping. They said, you know what, I need about four or five days to check it out and give me a call next week. You call them back next week. They don't buy then. Are you going to schedule a callback or you can say, you know what, how about this? I send you the information. I'll send you another email, some other additional stuff. When you're ready, give me a callback. I'd much rather have you do that than waste your time keeping calling back. And calling back and calling back because all you're doing is you're stopping yourself from doing the obvious, which is pick up the phone and start dialing fresh leaps. You get it? So it annoys me when I see some of this stuff out there in sales, you know, the average person doesn't buy into the seven cents. It's just nonsense. It's true in some industries and very un-srewin' others. So once you know what your sales cycle is, that's step one. Then once you've established how many calls is appropriate before you kind of just put it in, let's say the dead file, which means it will be recirculated maybe six months later, depending on this, again, depending on the situation, right? You would just throw it away. You put it in a file, this callback in a year, callback in six months, right? Maybe you put them into a drip campaign, meaning you can send them some emails, nurturing them and then circle back around that maybe six months or a year later. But don't get caught up in NLC calling people because it's going to destroy you. And also, you know, because A, waste your time and B, it just, it's almost like it forces you or allows you to be weak on the phone. Because as long as you think in your mind that, you know, I have this chance to call back again, you're just not going to have that certainty in your voice and that fortitude to kind of push it to its ultimate conclusion, either they buy or don't buy. Now, I'm not trying to say it should be in the first to second call, but sooner rather than later. OK. So now, flipping to the other side of the equation, I've worked in companies with really long sales cycles where you're dealing with things like, you know, health and wellness in real estate was a perfect example where you'd have to meet with someone three or four times and then meet with their electricians and plumbers because it was all these different people that had to agree and be in on the process and give sign-offs. That was a six month sale cycle there where you would call back seven or eight times and it would be appropriate. So you got to know your customer. That's number one. Then going forward, how do you follow up? Well, the biggest mistake people make is they only follow up using one modality. They call on the phone. They keep calling on the phone and calling on the phone. The greatest follow up technique in the world was invented not by email, by text. Text is to me the greatest way to follow up. Text and especially WhatsApp, if you're going international, people always look at their text, they always respond pretty much unless you're really low on tone, email not quite as much. So I mean, I'm at the point now, my email is almost like something that is a secondary thing to me. My texts are really my near term things that always get in my face. So what you want to be doing is combining your follow up with multiple modalities. You want to be calling them. You want to be emailing them. You want to be texting them, especially combining those things, sending them information that's relevant. So throughout that follow up cycle, it's not just on call every day two o'clock, right? And then you get the same result every day to a clock, right? Now I went through some of this in the gatekeeper training in terms of, you know, using different methodologies to get past the gatekeeper. But that's really before you picked the person follow up in this context is about what happens after you've actually made your presentations to the decision maker, they still haven't bought. How do you then go about getting in touch with them again and again and representing and so forth? So that's the ticket. So let's say in those cases where it does actually make sense to follow up. Is there a one size fits all strategy that people need to follow or does it need to be tailored based on your industry? What you're selling? Right. Yeah. Well, obviously it needs to be tailored to your industry. So what I would do is the first step is to say, okay, how many times does it make sense to follow up? What is my dead point where it goes into the pile that gets resurgulated at some point down the road, right? Second, what is the best cadence for lack of the word to how often should I reach out? What should be my schedule of touch points? So what you want to do there is you want to map it out deliberately, meaning I'm going to make my pitch happens on day one. My follow up email goes out day one, three hours later. My text comes out the next day. My call back comes two days later. In other words, we'll have a picture. And in the exercise area, I'm going to put some stuff there, different examples of cadences and say, which one is yours or fill in the blanks to have you create what is your best cadence of following up? Because again, as you said, it's not one size fits all in a longer sale cycle with this multiple decision makers, you're going to need to have time and space between your calls. So what you want to be doing during those periods where you're not actually speaking, you're waiting for people to go out and get other decision makers to go out, you want to be sending information via email, making them more certain, you could have schmooze calls in there, even how you're doing just touch points in there, but you're not aggressive trying to file with a closing call. So again, it's about understanding what industry and having the proper cadence. And then also in terms of, you know, what are the best times to follow up? In other words, very much like getting to a gatekeeper, you'll find that with emails. Emails have sort of peak days and times when they get read and when they don't get read, texts are the same thing. There are certain times with telephone calls, like when telephone calls typically get answered in the case of the phone. You know, if you're calling a CEO and you're calling every day at two o'clock, it's going to be difficult. You want to call late early on weekends, even at nighttime, you'll find that the CEOs work off hours while their gatekeepers work set hours. But again, I think in the context of following up after the initial presentation, the most frustrating thing for any sales person is that, and this is really the million dollar question, is that what do you do with that person who seems so interested? They just seem so interested when you're on the phone, they were this close and you can't ever get them back on the phone a second time. Those are the ones that are really annoying a salesperson. And that's really when these strategies can make a difference. When you start emailing them things, you can actually text them. You could also, by the way, if it's a CEO, you could actually email us to your vice president, email people, they speak. People within a company speak. So you want to be working on other people in the decision chain as well. Even if it's not the decision maker, but people will input will be valued and you want to be making them certain as well. So it could be also going up the ladder, down the ladder as you follow up, getting other people in the company on board as well. And it's almost like the same philosophy as advertising. You know, sometimes you say, well, you know, my branded advertising, it doesn't really have a call to action. I don't know whether it's working or not. And that's truly we never really know. But what they say is that you want someone to get a call to action. They see it, it says buy, they don't buy. They hear it better on the radio casually. They watch it on TV. So mentions it at the water cooler. They see it online. It's everywhere. Like, they start to see a message again and again and again, not necessarily with a call to action, but they just see something about what it is that you're selling and it raises their awareness. So finally, they almost get worn down. It's why when you advertise on the radio, they would say, well, you have to advertise for at least three months. That's probably part of the salesman trying to get your advertising. Most of that people need to hear something again and again until they finally feel comfortable to buy. So all of these things go into the follow up equation. Let me give you a list of 10 things, maybe nine things to follow. All right. So these are for you directly. So number one, when you're following up, you always want to be using a schedule. Don't leave follow up to random chance. All right. Very easy nowadays with online counters and your phone and whatever CRM you're using. Right. So you want to make sure that you're calling based on a predetermined schedule that's been honed to near perfection for your industry to trial and error through what other people are doing. You see when are people most likely devising the third call, fourth call and don't keep calling beyond that. You have to you reach this point diminishing returns. So that's number one. Okay. You want to have a follow up schedule. Number two, okay. You want to use different formats for following up. You're not just going to keep calling. You're not just going to keep texting. You're going to use emails. You're going to use text. You're going to use the telephone. You can even use regular snail mail where you have to send someone a brochure. You want to call above them, below them. You want to use all these different methodologies. So the sum total of everything you do, just says, oh, my God, I keep hearing about this. What is this thing I keep hearing about? That's the second thing. All right. Number three, okay. You want to make sure that as you're figuring out your schedule, you're timing it. I mean, what times of days are you reaching out? Time it correctly. If you time it the same time every day, you're not going to end up really having that much, much of an impact. But if you time it so that it's nine o'clock in the morning, one time, noon another day, six p.m. Another day, eight p.m. A weekend, you'll have a lot more success. They simply put the numbers game. People work at different times. They're secretaries or gatekeepers leave it other times. So that's another one. Okay. So now another good one. Okay. Is when you're sending out emails after that matter of texts, make sure that you develop templates for this. In other words, what you want to do is don't keep writing the same email from scratch and keep changing it. Find your best way of saying something, form a template and then just change a few things so it matches what the person's needs are, what the conversation has been. But make sure that the bulk of what you're writing becomes a template just to save yourself time. You don't want this to sound canned, obviously, because of one size fits all, but certainly 90 plus percent of your email can be templated. Okay. That's another one. Now here is a biggie and this goes for all aspects of selling. There's an old saying, what isn't tracked, what isn't measured, doesn't get improved. Right. Make sure that you track this stuff. This is absolutely crucial. If you don't track your results, then you really have no basis for testing to see what you should do, what you should do. I'm a big believer in A, B split testing, meaning, you know, you try calling every other day, every third day, try calling four times, six times, test two different groups where one group gets a call every day, one group gets a call every third day, one group gets called four times, one group gets called eight times. Keep track, see which one's outperforming which. That's the only surefire way to hone a followup procedure to perfection by testing. Otherwise, it's guesswork, it's emotional, doesn't really work. Okay. Now, another point, one of the things you want to do is that when you actually do get someone on the phone, you want to be as specific as possible about what the next step is. Meaning, leave with the preterm plan is, I'm going to call you back if possible on a specific day, a specific time, if that's in your best interest. Again, sometimes if your hit ratio is very low, if you're calling people back and 99.4% of them never buy from you, well, I'm not going to want you instituting all those callbacks rather I would say, listen, when you're ready, give me a call back. Now, if we can speak on Tuesday, give me a call back. Whatever, you're not going to actually waste your time making the call because the hit ratio is too low. Now you know when someone does call you back, you probably have a really interested person on your hand. But whatever the case, you want to always have some predetermined outcome. Let's get what's the outcome of the call? What's the next step? When will you speak next? When will you touch base next? That's important. Okay. Now, in addition, another step, as you're going through this process, as it keeps extending and extending, you want to keep offering them content. In other words, educate them, keep sending them things, testimonials, random articles with handwritten notes and a little yellow, I love the little yellow postage stuff. Hey, I thought you'd like this where you start sending them things that you think will help them make a positive decision. So use the power of email and regular mail too, but send your prospect things to use to educate them throughout the follow up process and also remember educate above and below as well. Very important. Okay. Now, in addition, along with tracking results overall, when it comes to emails, what you'll find is if you're using a CRM, this very sophisticated software that tracks open rates, how people are reading something, and you could start out, you know, kind of fooling around with your headlines, altering your headlines, doing A, B splits on what are people opening, what aren't they opening? Make sure you track your email results because you're going to find that if you test two emails, one word is changed in the headline and twice as many people open it. So also, I want you to get that granular with your follow up campaigns. All right. And then lastly here, okay, is make sure that you're always operating under this overarching equation of am I just doing this because I don't want to call call new people. Collective wisdom. You don't want people just burning through new leads without milking the existing ones. And while that's true, it's a very myopic way of going through life. Okay. As a salesperson, in other words, most salespeople, given the chance will avoid cold calling. I've seen this in every industry. They avoid cold calling. They will milk their old leads that are pretty much already dead. They'll milk them to death unless some discipline is put on the system. So always ask yourself, am I following up because I don't want to be calling new people, only dialing that phone and knocking on doors, or I really have a shot here to close a set. So those are really the steps to following up effectively. I'm going to supply you with guys with some templates in the back of what you would actually say on these calls, just ways to start, how you reintroduce yourself. You know, one of the things I always do well when I follow up with like, hey, you know, you know, a couple of reasons for the call. Number one, want to give you a quick update on something. And then second reason for the cause, then you go into the close. So I'll include some of these in the exercise area, but you know, that's pretty much it guys. You know, when it comes to following up, the key is, is that while you don't want to become just a cold calling machine where you let these leads just dying on the vine, don't think for a second that the secret to success is to become a massive follow up. Okay. I've seen other systems of training when they say, you got to just be overly relentless and crazy with follow up. And you know what? That's kind of nuts. Cause if no one's buying from you after the third call, if that's the real, it's very often a reality. Is it the third or fourth call? The numbers just go spiraling down. You need to know what's that spiral down point. What's that point in the process where it's diminishing returns and stop. Don't just keep calling and calling and calling and following cause you want to look busy and show that your core logs are, you know, are very robust. So the magic, oh, look how he's really good. You're not going to make any money that way. And eventually, you know, it'll be a short lived career. All right. There we go. So how do you know when it's time to stop following up and throw away a lead? I'm not a big believer in throwing away leads. So you want to put these leads into a pile. It's going to recirculation pile. It may be six months or a year later, they get recirculated. People can call and people circumstances change, right? But here's what I would say the best way to really handle this in reality. That be, you know, diving into each of your businesses and telling you, this is the point, right? Is track your results and find out what is that point after X number of calls. When does the results just go off a cliff? There'll be that point. There's going to be this one point where like, look, I think in this statistic, I think in the statistics, they say, you know, 90% of the people will call up to the sixth call. 90% of the people will close up to that sixth call. And then after that sixth call, it goes to like 0.5% will buy, right? Well, that's an industry average that I don't really believe is true. And also part of the reason for that is because they're not using the straight line. And when you're not using the straight line, you're not creating enough certainty. So you're forced to spread it out of them more calls. When you're looping correctly, one calls like three calls, because you're creating more certainty within a call. So somebody's exaggeratedly long sales cycles because the sales people are weak. They're not well trained. So when you train a salesperson effectively, what happens is you can collapse that time. But again, I was just going to be that spot where you watch the charges go and it collapses. That's your point. Okay. And just remember this. Just remember this. All right. Your mindset on the phone call, what in person is that I want to close the person right here, don't allow a follow up campaign as important as it is. It might be right. Don't allow a follow up campaign. It's important as it might be to cause you to essentially lose your spine when you're there because you know it. Okay. I don't want to piss the person off. I don't want to get in their face and I'll just wait to follow up. Here's the thing. Yeah, you never ever want to piss someone off. You never ever want to get in their face, but you don't have to. If you're using the right tonalities, the right body language and the patterns I taught you in looping about don't misconstru my enthusiasm pressure, you know exactly how to navigate these loops in the back end, where to stop, when to set up a call back, when to have them call you back. All right. I think one final point that most people will never think of is, do you want to confirm appointments on these callbacks? Let's say you're out there in the field. Do you want to actually confirm the appointment? Or do you just show up? Good question, right? The answer is you got to test them. You got to test both. But I will tell you this one thing I have found. I was doing, you know, more just as you know, right? And I had salesmen to complain, oh my God. And, you know, I went away to stat. I don't know if the person wasn't there. I want to start, you know, making sure people are always there. And I say, well, you know, that's okay. But think of it this way. If you show up to someone's house in stat island and they're not there, how bad do they feel afterwards? When you call them, they feel really bad. I said, well, if you actually say, oh my God, I drove all out there, you know what? The chance of you closing that person on the reschedule are massive because people feel bad about it. So don't just dismiss it out of hand. Like I got a schedule to make sure they're there to confirm. When you confirm, it gives them a chance to cancel. Now I'm not saying that you don't want to confirm. If you have to drive a really long way and that's the only appointment you have in that area for they to blow your day. Yes, you'd want to confirm. I test it. But if you're in a situation where there's lots of other business to be done in that area, you show up somewhere and they're not there and you made the trip. That person is going to be so much more receptive. They'll feel bad about it. There's a marker, unconscious marker. And once you have that, you don't want to use it to disempower some, but you have that and that sort of sets the stage for a really easy close the next time around. All right, your back, pretty awesome stuff, right? I told you, this is really nothing to sneeze at. Following up can increase your clothes rate by no more 30 to 50% depending on the product you're selling. So you got to employ these strategies religiously, diligently, and also selectively. Remember, there's a balance between following up and following up because you don't want to make more cold calls or knock on more doors or just go visiting more businesses out in the field. So you want to follow up correctly, do it the right way. And it promise you in conjunction with the power of looping, which truly allows you now as you can see to close anyone's closeable, you've set yourself up for a massive greatness.
